transition.title: The same map, a different measure
transition.body: You've seen how many people each disaster reached. Now watch what it cost. The same nations, re-scaled — this time by the share of their economy lost in a single event.

economicSteps.title: Vanuatu — the heaviest blow
economicSteps.body: A single cyclone erased just over 10 percent of Vanuatu's entire economy in one year — the steepest economic loss any Pacific nation recorded in the period. For an economy this size, a year of growth can vanish in an afternoon.

economicSteps.title: Fiji — Winston's bill
economicSteps.body: Cyclone Winston cost Fiji more than 7 percent of GDP — over 370 million dollars in a single event. The same storm that reached two-thirds of Fiji's people also took a sum its economy would spend years recovering.

economicSteps.title: Size is its own protection
economicSteps.body: The pattern inverts for the largest economy. Papua New Guinea's worst disaster cost a twentieth of one percent of its GDP — not because it was spared, but because a large economy absorbs what a small one cannot. The smallest nations carry the heaviest economic blows for the same reason they carry the heaviest human ones: there is less to absorb the shock.

outro.title: Two measures, one pattern
outro.body: Whether counted in people or in dollars, the same kind of nation absorbs the most — and absorbs it relative to how little it has to begin with. The losses are not random. They trace a fault line that runs deeper than any single storm: not in what disasters do, but in what each nation is able to withstand.
